description: Appearance, sensory traits, blood type, and physical characteristics

rs17602729.name: Myoadenylate deaminase (AMPD1 gene)

G/G.interpretation: Your AMPD1 genotype is the most common form in reference populations. This does not rule out myoadenylate deaminase (ampd1 gene) risk from other genes, environmental exposures, or rare variants not covered by standard genotyping.
recommendations: No specific AMPD1-directed intervention indicated, but standard health screening still applies
Other genetic and environmental factors contribute to myoadenylate deaminase (ampd1 gene) — genotype alone is not the full picture

GG.interpretation: For myoadenylate deaminase (ampd1 gene), your AMPD1 variant is one contributing factor — no c34t loss-of-function allele was detected at this marker
recommendations: Use actual training response and performance tests to guide programming

AG.interpretation: For myoadenylate deaminase (ampd1 gene), your AMPD1 variant is one contributing factor — one loss-of-function-associated allele was observed
recommendations: Compare symptoms with workload, sleep, fueling, iron status, and measured recovery
Seek clinical evaluation for persistent or disproportionate exercise intolerance

AA.interpretation: For myoadenylate deaminase (ampd1 gene), your AMPD1 variant is one contributing factor — two loss-of-function-associated alleles can reduce skeletal-muscle amp deaminase activity, but symptoms and exercise capacity vary This is a statistical association that interacts with lifestyle, environment, and other genetic factors.
recommendations: Discuss persistent exercise intolerance, cramps, or unusual fatigue with a qualified clinician
Use symptom-guided pacing while other common causes are assessed

bibliography: Del Coso J, Lucia A. Genetic Influence in Exercise Performance. Genes (Basel). 2021 Apr 27;12(5):651.
Urtizberea JA, Severa G, Malfatti E. Metabolic Myopathies in the Era of Next-Generation Sequencing.
Leońska-Duniec A, Maculewicz E, Humińska-Lisowska K, et al. AMPD1 C34T Polymorphism (rs17602729)
Is Not Associated with Post-Exercise Changes of Body Weight, Body Composition, and Biochemical
Parameters in Caucasian Females. Genes (Basel). 2020 May 16;11(5):558.
Lopez-Schenk R, Collins NL, Schenk NA, Beard DA. Integrated Functions of Cardiac Energetics, Mechanics,
and Purine Nucleotide Metabolism. Compr Physiol. 2023 Dec 29;14(1):5345-5369.
Noury JB, Zagnoli F, Petit F, et al. Exercise efficiency impairment in metabolic myopathies. Sci Rep. 2020
Rannou F, Scotet V, Marcorelles P, Monnoyer R, Le Maréchal C. Effects of AMPD1 common mutation on the
metabolic-chronotropic relationship: Insights from patients with myoadenylate deaminase deficiency.
rs17602729.technical_report:
The AMPD1 gene produces the enzyme adenosine monophosphate deaminase 1 or
myoadenylate deaminase in skeletal muscle where it plays a relevant role in metabolism by
catalyzing the deamination of adenosine monophosphate to inosine monophosphate and
promoting ATP resynthesis (Del Coso and Lucia, 2021). For many years, MAD has been considered a myopathy of metabolic nature. The c.34C>T
or p.Gln12Ter (rs17602729) variant in the AMPD1 gene is the best known and produces
a stop codon that results in a truncated protein and a reduction in myoadenylate
deaminase activity. The c.34C>T variant has an allele frequency of 12% in the non-Finnish European population. Only a minority of carriers of two copies of c.34C>T develop deficiency-related myopathy
so that the c.34C>T variant is currently not considered pathogenic, but as a variant of
uncertain significance. The information on the c.34C>T variant should be interpreted with caution because,
although initially considered pathogenic, it has a considerable frequency in the general
population and it is believed that this deficiency is compensated by other metabolic
pathways (Urtizberea et al., 2023). On the other hand, there are studies suggesting that increased levels of c.34C>T adenosine
could have a cardioprotective effect and have observed a correlation between the c.34C>T
genotype and a better evolution of patients with heart failure, coronary artery disease,
ischemic cardiomyopathy and cardiac transplantation (Rannou et al., 2017; Lopez-Schenk et
al., 2023).
rs17602729.description:
PDF

Myoadenylate deaminase (AMPD1 gene)
Myoadenylate deaminase deficiency is one of the most common causes of metabolic
myopathy. It affects 1 to 2% of the European population, although in most people it does
not cause any symptoms.

rs4481887.name: Asparagus odor detection

G/G.interpretation: Your OR2M7 genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to asparagus odor detection. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of OR2M7 in asparagus odor detection
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Pelchat ML, Bykowski C, Duke FF, Reed DR. Excretion and Perception of a Characteristic Odor in Urine after
Asparagus Ingestion: a Psychophysical and Genetic Study. Chem Senses, 2011; 36(1):917.
Eriksson, Nicholas et al. Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for
common traits. PLoS genetics vol. 6,6 e1000993. 24 Jun. 2010.
rs4481887.technical_report:
It appears that the ability to detect asparagus odor in urine, produced by chemical
compounds metabolized after ingestion, is related to a single nucleotide polymorphism near
the OR2M7 gene, which codes for an olfactory receptor. This genotype-phenotype

 relationship is similar to that of other olfactory receptor alleles that reduce the ability to
smell androstenone and isovaleric acid. The polymorphism is found within a large group of
olfactory receptors on chromosomal region 1q44. OR2M7 itself responds to other odorant
compounds such as geraniol and cintrone. This result is important because it informs us about a possible anosmia, i.e., a specific loss
of the sense of smell due to loss of function in one or more olfactory receptors.
rs4481887.description:
Asparagus odor detection
After ingesting asparagus, some people can detect an unmistakable aroma in their urine.
Not all people have this ability, which is influenced by genetics.

rs8176747.name: Blood Group ABO/Rh

C/C.interpretation: Your ABO genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to blood group abo/rh. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of ABO in blood group abo/rh
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Dean L. Blood Groups and Red Cell Antigens. National Center for Biotechnology Information [May 2022].
ABO blood group system. Encyclopedia Britannica, [May 2022]
National Health Services. Blood Groups [May 2022]
rs8176747.technical_report:
The anchoring of antigens on the surface of red blood cells that determines the blood group
depends on the genetics of each individual. Based on several studies, 4 markers associated
with antigen production have been identified. These polymorphisms are found in the ABO
and RHD genes. The ABO gene codes for a differential glycosyltransferase enzyme that
catalyzes the addition of the corresponding monosaccharide on the so-called H antigen, a
molecule present on the surface of red blood cells from which the A and B antigens are
formed. The A antigen is produced by an enzyme that adds a molecule of N-acetyl
galactosamine to the H antigen, while the B antigen is produced by an enzyme that adds a
D-galactose to the H antigen. On the other hand, the ability to produce the Rh antigen is
determined by the genotype in the RHD gene. This gene is also linked to the RHCE gene, so
that their genotypes are inherited together and both can be used to determine the Rh group. IMPORTANT: With this analysis we make a prediction of a person's blood type thanks to the
genetic variants described for each blood group. The reliability of this technique is 90%, so
there can be cases (10%) in which genetics do not match the blood group. Therefore, to
know exactly your blood type, you must perform a blood biochemical analysis.
rs8176747.description:
Blood Group ABO/Rh
The blood group of each individual is determined by the surface proteins expressed on the
membrane of the red blood cells. Of the multitude of systems described, the most relevant,
especially for human-to-human compatibility, are the ABO and Rh systems.

rs3827760.name: Earlobe type

A/A.interpretation: Your EDAR genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to earlobe type. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of EDAR in earlobe type
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Shaffer JR, Li J, Lee MK, Roosenboom J, Orlova E, Adhikari K, et al. Multiethnic GWAS Reveals Polygenic
Architecture of Earlobe Attachment. Am J Hum Genet, 2017; 101(6):91324.
Adhikari K, Reales G, Smith AJP, Konka E, Palmen J, Quinto-Sanchez M, et al. A genome-wide association
study identifies multiple loci for variation in human ear morphology. Nat Commun, 2015; 6(1):7500.
rs3827760.technical_report:
The degree of separation of the earlobe from the head is a polygenic trait (depends on
several genes) and is not an "all or nothing" genetic trait, but rather there is a continuous
distribution and there are degrees of earlobe attachment.tically responds to an "all or
nothing", but rather there is a continuous distribution and there are degrees of earlobe
attachment. Furthermore, it is a trait that is highly determined by the geographic ancestry of
the individual. Based on a genome-wide association study (GWAS) involving 75,000 individuals of different
geographic ancestries, 3 markers associated with the likelihood of having the earlobe more
or less separated from the rest of the head have been identified. The identified markers are found in genes such as the EDAR gene, which produces the
ectodysplasin A receptor protein, which plays an important role in the development of
ectodermal tissues such as skin; the ITGA8 gene, related to wound healing and
organogenesis; and the ADAMTS9-AS2 gene, used as a possible marker in the development
of some types of cancers. The study of this trait is important because on certain occasions this phenotypic trait may
be associated with certain developmental disorders and malformations (e.g., defects in the
jaw musculature and defects in the morphology of the jaw).The study of this trait is
important because the genes that are associated with the type of earlobe a person has are
also involved in development.
rs3827760.description:
Earlobe type
The separation of the earlobe from the skin of the head is a polygenic trait that is largely
influenced by the genetic factor and the origin of the individual.

rs17822931.name: Earwax type / Armpit odor

C/T.interpretation: Your ABCC11 genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to earwax type / armpit odor. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of ABCC11 in earwax type / armpit odor
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Yoshiura K, Kinoshita A, Ishida T, Ninokata A, Ishikawa T, Kaname T, et al. A SNP in the ABCC11 gene is the
determinant of human earwax type. Nat Genet, 2006; 38(3):32430.
Prokop-Prigge KA, Mansfield CJ, Parker MR, Thaler E, Grice EA, Wysocki CJ, et al. Ethnic/racial and genetic
influences on cerumen odorant profiles. J Chem Ecol, 2015; 41(1):6774.
Ohashi J, Naka I, Tsuchiya N. The Impact of Natural Selection on an ABCC11 SNP Determining Earwax
Type. Mol Biol Evol, 2011; 28(1):84957.
Martin A, Saathoff M, Kuhn F, Max H, Terstegen L, Natsch A. A functional ABCC11 allele is essential in the
biochemical formation of human axillary odor. J Invest Dermatol, 2010; 130(2):52940.
Harker M, Carvell A-M, Marti VPJ, Riazanskaia S, Kelso H, Taylor D, et al. Functional characterisation of a
SNP in the ABCC11 allele - effects on axillary skin metabolism, odour generation and associated
behaviours. J Dermatol Sci, 2014; 73(1):2330.
rs17822931.technical_report:
The type of cerumen and the intensity of axillary odor are related traits influenced by
genetic factors. Several studies have determined the presence of a marker in the ABCC11

 gene associated with these characteristics and whose allelic frequencies are strongly
determined by the geographic ancestry of the individual. Thus, the different genotypes of the marker found have been associated with a dry,
colorless and crumbly cerumen, or a moist, dark and sticky cerumen, respectively. On the other hand, the genotype found associated with a moist cerumen has also been
associated with a higher production of volatile substances, which translates into a cerumen
with a higher amount of aromatic substances and a higher body odor through the secretions
of the axillae. The ABCC11 gene encodes for a cellular transporter found in the membranes of various cell
types and is involved in the transport of small molecules as well as external agents (e.g. a
variety of drugs). The ABCC1 transporter is found in the membranes of the cells that secrete
cerumen in the ear as well as in the cells of the glands that secrete sweat in the armpits,
hence the relationship between the presence of polymorphisms in this gene - the type of
wax - the type of sweat.
rs17822931.description:
PDF

Earwax type / Armpit odor
The intensity of axillary odor and the type of cerumen that people have are interrelated
traits, since they share elements between them, such as their transporter in the membranes
of the cells where these traits are manifested.

rs7559271.name: Nasion prominence

A/A.interpretation: Your PAX3 genotype is the most common form in reference populations. This does not rule out nasion prominence risk from other genes, environmental exposures, or rare variants not covered by standard genotyping.
recommendations: No specific PAX3-directed intervention indicated, but standard health screening still applies
Other genetic and environmental factors contribute to nasion prominence — genotype alone is not the full picture

bibliography: Liu F et al. A genome-wide association study identifies five loci influencing facial morphology in
Europeans. Gibson G, editor. PLoS Genet, 2012; 8(9): e1002932.
Paternoster L et al. Genome-wide association study of three-dimensional facial morphology identifies a
variant in PAX3 associated with nasion position. Am J Hum Genet, 2012; 90(3):47885.
rs7559271.technical_report:
Twin, family, and animal evidence suggests that heredity plays an important role in
determining craniofacial morphology. Several genome-wide association studies have
identified 1 marker associated with nasion prominence in people. This marker is found in
the PAX3 gene. PAX3 encodes a developmentally important transcription factor that is
expressed in neural crest cells, which contribute to most differentiated cell types of the
vertebrate face. In humans, moreover, PAX3 is one of six genes mutated in Waardenburg
syndrome, which is characterized by a number of neural crest-related phenotypes, including
minor facial dysmorphia manifested as a broad nasal root and increased distance between
the medial canthi or corners of the eye.
rs7559271.description:
Nasion prominence
Facial morphology is one of the most complex and variable features of each individual that
determine part of the physical identity of the face. Part of this physical identity is
determined by the degree of prominence of the nasion, the bony fusion point of the nasal
bones and the frontal bone of each person.

rs2281845.name: Permanent tooth eruption

C/C.interpretation: Your CACNA1S genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to permanent tooth eruption. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of CACNA1S in permanent tooth eruption
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Geller F et al. Genome-Wide Association Study Identifies Four Loci Associated with Eruption of Permanent
Teeth. PLoS Genet. 2011;7(9):e1002275.
WebMD. Dental Health and your childrens teeth [May 2022]
Kurosaka H et al. Development of dentition: From initiation to occlusion and related diseases. J Oral
rs2281845.technical_report:
Many dental traits are known to be substantially influenced by genetic factors. The time it
takes for permanent teeth to erupt is thought to be highly heritable and may have important
implications for the risk of malocclusion, crowding, and periodontal disease. Based on a
genome-wide association study, 4 markers associated with tooth eruption have been
identified. Delayed tooth eruption was also associated with the production of fewer
permanent teeth. Among the genes that condition the eruption of permanent teeth we can
find the CACNA1S gene or the ADK gene.
rs2281845.description:
Permanent tooth eruption
Dental maturation is the process that includes the loss of primary teeth and the eruption
and calcification of permanent teeth. This process of tooth replacement begins in infancy
and ends practically at the onset of puberty and can be influenced by numerous variables
that determine both the time elapsed and the tooth structure.

rs4491709.name: Permanent tooth eruption

recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of LOC101928278 in permanent tooth eruption
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Geller F et al. Genome-Wide Association Study Identifies Four Loci Associated with Eruption of Permanent
Teeth. PLoS Genet. 2011;7(9):e1002275.
WebMD. Dental Health and your childrens teeth [May 2022]
Kurosaka H et al. Development of dentition: From initiation to occlusion and related diseases. J Oral

rs10427255.name: Photic sneeze reflex

T/T.interpretation: Your 2q22.3 genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to photic sneeze reflex. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of 2q22.3 in photic sneeze reflex
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Eriksson N, Macpherson JM, Tung JY, Hon LS, Naughton B, Saxonov S, et al. Web-based, participant-driven
studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits. PLoS Genet. 2010;6(6):120.
Wang M, Sun X, Shi Y, Song X, Mi H. A genome-wide association study on photic sneeze reflex in the
Chinese population. Sci Rep. 2019 Mar 21;9(1):4993.
rs10427255.technical_report:
The fact of responding involuntarily with a sneeze when exposed to bright light is a trait that
possibly follows an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance, although studies are
inconclusive and suggest that more genes can be involved. The genome-wide association study carried out by Eriksson et al. suggests that this trait is
associated with a region of chromosome 2 (2q22.3), close to the ZEB2 gene. research indicates that certain variants with a low frequency in the general population
found in the ZEB2 gene produce Mowat-Wilson syndrome, which manifests with seizures
and photosensitivity, among other symptoms. This suggests that there can be a
relationship between photosensitive epileptic seizures and the photic sneeze reflex.
rs10427255.description:
Photic sneeze reflex
The explanation why some people tend to sneeze when exposed to bright light remains an
enigma. There are indications that certain variants may increase vulnerability to this
particular trait

rs12896399.name: Pigmented rings on the iris

T/T.interpretation: Your 14q32.12 genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to pigmented rings on the iris. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of 14q32.12 in pigmented rings on the iris
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Larsson M, et al. GWAS findings for human iris patterns: associations with variants in genes that influence
normal neuronal pattern development. Am J Hum Genet. 2011 Aug 12;89(2):334-43.
Mackey DA, Wilkinson CH, Kearns LS, Hewitt AW. Classification of iris colour: review and refinement of a
classification schema. Clin Exp Ophthalmol. 2011 Jul;39(5):462-71.
rs12896399.technical_report:
It has been estimated that the structure and patterning of iris development can be inherited
up to 70-95%, so genetics is a major contributor to the expression of its associated traits. Through a GWAS study of 2600 individuals of European ancestry, the role of a marker close
to the SLC24A4 gene has been identified, which correlates with the development
of a ring of pigmentation in the iris, surrounding the pupil, which depends on the amount of
melanin surrounding the pupil, being the ring uniform or uneven. The results of the study further reported that melanin pigment rings surrounding the pupil
were smaller in males than in females, while females had a greater number of crypts, oval
shapes formed in the iris as a function of eye development.
rs12896399.description:
PDF

Pigmented rings on the iris
The iris is the colored part of the eye that surrounds the pupil. As with fingerprints, iris color
patterns are very complex and unique to each individual.

rs1805007.name: Probability of having red hair
rs1805007.display: MC1R - Probability of having red hair

C/C.interpretation: Your MC1R genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to probability of having red hair. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of MC1R in probability of having red hair
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Liu F et al. Colorful DNA polymorphisms in humans. Semin Cell Dev Biol. 2013;24(6-7):562-75.
Ito S et al. Diversity of human hair pigmentation as studied by chemical analysis of eumelanin and
pheomelanin. J Eur Acad Dermatol Venereol. 2011;25(12):1369-80.
Eriksson N et al. Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits.
Han J et al. A genome-wide association study identifies novel alleles associated with hair color and skin
pigmentation. PLoS Genet. 2008;4(5).
Sulem P et al. Two newly identified genetic determinants of pigmentation in Europeans. Nat Genet.
rs1805007.technical_report:
Hair color is a highly heritable trait with a complex genetic architecture. It is a trait whose
frequency is greatly affected by the geographical origin of the individual. Four markers
associated with the probability of having red hair color have been identified from studies
involving people of European ancestry, including genome-wide association studies (GWAS). These markers are found in the MC1R gene, a gene that produces the melanocortin 1
receptor, a protein involved in the development of skin color and hair color in mammals from
melanin synthesis by melanocytes. It is estimated that approximately 70% of people with
two variants in the MCR1 gene have red hair.
rs1805007.description:
Probability of having red hair
Red hair color is the result of the combination of two types of melanin pigments in specific
amounts, which ultimately determines the characteristic orange color. In addition, this
characteristic production of melanin pigments can also determine the color of the skin or
even the eyes together.

rs6591536.name: Smell

A/A.interpretation: Your OR5A1 genotype is one of several genetic factors linked to smell. Research in this area continues to evolve, and most traits have multiple genetic and environmental contributors.
recommendations: Stay informed as new research clarifies the role of OR5A1 in smell
Consider discussing with a healthcare provider familiar with genetic interpretation

bibliography: Jaeger SR, McRae JF, Bava CM, Beresford MK, Hunter D, Jia Y, et al. A mendelian trait for
olfactory sensitivity affects odor experience and food selection. Curr Biol [Internet]. Elsevier Ltd;
Aloum L, Alefishat E, Adem A, Petroianu G. Ionone Is More than a Violet's Fragrance: A Review. Molecules.
rs6591536.technical_report:
The ability to determine to a greater or lesser extent b-ionone compounds is strongly
conditioned by genetics. Several studies have determined that the only major region of
genetic association for b-ionone sensitivity discovered so far is located on human
chromosome 11, in the OR5A1 gene, which mediates producing the olfactory
receptor 5A1 that initiates a neuronal response that triggers odor perception. Individuals carrying genotypes for b-ionone can more easily differentiate between olfactory
stimuli and foods and beverages with and without added b-ionone. These responses

 associated with the genotypes of the marker found in the OR5A1 gene were confirmed in
96% of the individuals who participated in the study conducted.
rs6591536.description:
Smell
People can discern thousands of odors, although there is great variability among people in
the ability to detect particular odors. Some of the differences in this detection ability are
believed to be genetically determined.
